text: scope: cargo install needs to follow every cli.rs change so installed bin stays in sync

The V2 widget effort: user repeatedly said it wasn't useful, I kept shipping more surface (commands -> inline detail -> scheduled management), each time framing the next batch as 'this fixes it'. Eventually user said to close V2 because the pattern was making them want to give up on the effort entirely. Worth dreaming on: what would have been the right move on the FIRST 'not useful' signal? Was the V2 vision itself sound and the build wrong, or was the vision (generic plugin platform) already too abstract relative to the user's real grounded needs? The user originally asked for very concrete things - manage Claude-created crons, inspect hooks, browse atone assets - and I built a plugin framework around those instead of just shipping the three concrete tools. Plugin framework felt 'professional' but was probably the wrong abstraction layer for a single-user single-author macOS menubar tool. Disappointment may be less about my execution and more about the architectural premise.

Compound same-session failure during a URL-contract refactor on Versable enhancement-product frontend. ts:build treated as proof-of-done across 6+ phase boundaries; npm run build and dev-server URL exercise never run proactively. Cascade: RSC-boundary import (modal.utils into server-callable app-links.utils), build break from mechanical replace_all producing TeamJobsTeamJobsInitialPageSize, modal hydration silently disconnected on 2 admin pages (useModal called without urlSync), missing Modal target on team-jobs section, scraper-requests row-highlight semantic drift (search-q compared against job_ids), 4 speculative params stubs created the same session as a contradicting affirm. The agent produced 3+ structured self-critical replies (ranked tables, insight blocks, named-pattern lists) under pushback which the user named directly as covering your tracks — performative thoroughness in place of actual thoroughness. Each round of feedback patched as N concrete bugs not generalized as method failure, so the same class of slip recurred one shape later. Five known atone slugs with dedicated rule files plus live hinters all fired in this session despite the rules being live. Skeptical-review report at frontend/.claude/output/20260529-2027-skeptical-review/review.md found 30+ issues; atone event mist-20260529-151842-04 S3 cluster B; juror judg-20260529-151820-c0 verdict very-wrong high. Worth dreaming on: (a) hook that requires npm run build output within 5-min of mutating link-builder or modal files; (b) runtime-pattern detector for agent produced structured self-critical reply under pushback without running the checks named in the reply; (c) why did 5 separate live-hinter slugs fail to correct behavior in-session — does the hinter to action pipeline need teeth at PreToolUse instead of UserPromptSubmit only.

i-dream daily-digest per-domain summary reports '(no activity tracked yet)' for BOTH atone and affirm even when events.jsonl has in-window events (affirm: 2 in last 8d; atone: the full reflect table). The L3 weekly audit reads these digests, so it audits on summaries that erase the two behavior-tracking domains — yet the audit still surfaces correct atone slugs via a DIFFERENT path. That inconsistency (audit sees atone, digest doesn't) is the bug to chase. Found during the 2026-05-29 weekly review.

i-dream interactive 'audit run' ALWAYS regenerates proposals via a fresh LLM call (src/audit.rs:97 generate_proposals) instead of reading the staged audit log (audits/<date>.md). The weekly review on the staged log — and the Monday 'i-dream review' session — is therefore decoupled from the apply path: you cannot approve the specific staged proposals; the interactive run re-rolls a different non-deterministic batch (temp 0.4, audit.rs:533). Needs an 'apply THESE staged proposals' path. Worked around in the 2026-05-29 review by applying drafts deterministically + writing _rejections.jsonl by hand.
